author: @C-M-E
text: If you ever find the need to go REALLY fast and have a few thousand laying around, fiber lasers are where all the really mind blowing stuff comes in. I've been heavily tempted as of late but have quite a lot of other projects that need tending, and unfortunately, more precedent on the spending budget. I've looked at these units and the Atom Stack variants, either of which seem very usable at a hobbyist level.

author: @newmonengineering
text: As a laser owner myself lightburn is worth the money. If you try it, you will probably buy it. It's dead simple to learn and it does everything in one package. The only other thing I use is fusion360. Between the two you can make just about anything you can imagine in very short time. Fusion 360 is for taking a technical drawing with specific dimensions and making the paths you need exactly the way you need them. Lightburn both controls the laser as well as allows you to bring in any art work and setting it up for engraving. You can take Clipart,  trace it, cut it out, engrave a picture with ease. The only thing I wish lightburn would do is allow a more technical drawing where you need say a hole in an exact position. You can draw a square or hexagon the exact size but adding a hole to that square in an exact place is an issue and not easily done unfortunately. You can get close but its painful. But not every project requires this so it really depends on what you are making. If you just want to put a picture on something lightburn does everything you need.. if you just want to cut a clip art to a particular size it can do it. But since it's not a full blown CAD it's hard to make a technical drawing.  But I only recommend it because of the fact that it is an all in one package that pretty much does everything you normally do all in one screen. No need for 3 different programs to export and import. It's all right there and it does it well. Most people in the community use it because of just how easy and powerful it is. Plus it allows cool things with a camera. You can mount a camera and calibrate it directly over your machine and you can then place art directly on an object where you need it. Very cool feature. Say you have a state shaped Keychain and you want to draw something on it, you can lay it all out on an overlay from the camera over your machine and it will do it exactly how you draw it.

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: Well done Dr. Smith. Perhaps Luke could do a video on (public domain) free software. I'm cheaper then you, having been in computers since 1985. I've seen what Microsoft did to Novel NDOS and Netscape. For compatibility, portability and exportability to different formats I use these: FreeCAD, GIMP and Open Office. Of course this is a laser cutting video, but, I find 3D printing, laser engraving and paper printing very similar. They are just slices you can stack. You just need to know the device file formats and which program will give you that output format you need. Stay away from proprietary devices. The public domain software(s) have a much richer community.
